summary: Gruber pushes back on Robert X. Cringely's narrative about Tony Fadell's departure from Apple, arguing that the real story is simpler and less conspiratorial than Cringely suggests. Papermaster was hired into a broader role than Fadell ever held, overseeing all handheld hardware engineering rather than just the iPod division. Gruber reveals that Fadell had little involvement with the iPhone, and that Fadell had actually pushed for using Linux rather than OS X for the phone — a debate he lost. Fadell's diminished position was a natural consequence of the iPhone eclipsing the iPod as Apple's flagship product. Gruber dismisses Cringely's theory that Jobs felt threatened by Fadell as absurd, noting that Jonathan Ive is far more prominent and has never been pushed out. The piece paints Fadell as someone whose enormous contributions were overtaken by the very platform he argued against building.
key_points: Papermaster's role as SVP of Devices Hardware Engineering is a superset of Fadell's former iPod-only role, encompassing all iPhone and iPod hardware
Fadell had very little involvement with the iPhone and even the iPod Touch was built by the iPhone team, not Fadell's division
Fadell pushed for using Linux for the iPhone rather than OS X — a debate he lost to Forstall and Serlet, in what Gruber calls possibly the best early design decision in Apple's history
The idea that Jobs felt threatened by Fadell is dismissed as goofy, since Jony Ive is far more prominent and has thrived at Apple
Fadell was never offered the expanded role; the iPhone eclipsed the iPod as Apple's A Team, and Fadell wasn't a B Team person
money_quotes: The iPhone has eclipsed the iPod as the A Team at Apple, and Tony Fadell does not sound like a B Team sort of guy.
Hyperbolic though it may sound, it may prove to be the single best early design decision in the entire history of the company.
It seems hard to imagine the iPhone any other way now, but at the outset it was not a foregone conclusion that a stripped down and revamped version of OS X would work for a mobile phone.
Steve Jobs is not insecure; if he were, he'd have canned someone like Jonathan Ive long before putting the squeeze to Tony Fadell.
For a company of Apple's size and success, the relative stability of its executive team is remarkable.
Steve Jobs is a good jerk, Tony Fadell is a bad jerk.
Fadell's ticket has probably been punched ever since the iPhone shipped and proved to be an enormous success.
key_insight: Fadell's exit wasn't palace intrigue — the iPhone's triumph over the iPod made his narrower role obsolete, especially after he lost the pivotal debate over which OS should power it.
